exercises.prompt: Crown the top movie in each genre. Over movies with at least 20 ratings and a non-null genre1, use ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) inside a CTE, then keep only the row numbered 1 in each genre. Return genre1, title, avg_rating, and num_ratings, ordered by genre1 ascending. (The extra ORDER BY keys inside the window break rating ties deterministically so exactly one champion is chosen per genre.)
exercises.referenceSql: WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS rn FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 20 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL) SELECT genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1 ORDER BY genre1
hints: A window function cannot live in WHERE, so compute ROW_NUMBER() in a CTE and filter on it in the outer query. PARTITION BY genre1 restarts the numbering for each genre; the ORDER BY inside OVER decides who gets number 1.

WITH ranked AS (SELECT ..., ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS rn FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 20 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL) SELECT genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1 ORDER BY genre1.

```sql
WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS rn FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 20 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL) SELECT genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1 ORDER BY genre1
```

exercises.prompt: List the top 3 movies in each of four genres. Over Action, Adventure, Crime, and Animation movies with at least 30 ratings, number them with ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) inside a CTE, then keep rows numbered 3 or lower. Return genre1, rn, title, avg_rating, and num_ratings, ordered by genre1 ascending, then rn ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS rn FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.genre1 IN ('Action','Adventure','Crime','Animation')) SELECT genre1, rn, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE rn <= 3 ORDER BY genre1, rn
hints: Same shape as crowning a single champion, but keep rn <= 3 instead of rn = 1. Because ROW_NUMBER assigns strictly distinct numbers, each genre yields exactly three rows even when ratings tie.

WITH ranked AS (SELECT ..., ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS rn FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.genre1 IN ('Action','Adventure','Crime','Animation')) SELECT genre1, rn, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE rn <= 3 ORDER BY genre1, rn.

```sql
WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS rn FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.genre1 IN ('Action','Adventure','Crime','Animation')) SELECT genre1, rn, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE rn <= 3 ORDER BY genre1, rn
```
collectibles: Spirited Away (Sen to Chihiro no kamikakushi)

exercises.prompt: See how RANK handles ties. Over Action, Crime, and Animation movies with at least 30 ratings, compute RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY avg_rating DESC) — note the window orders by avg_rating ONLY, so movies with equal ratings share a rank and the next rank is skipped. Keep rows with rank 3 or lower. Return genre1, rnk, title, avg_rating, and num_ratings, ordered by genre1 ascending, then rnk ascending, then num_ratings descending, then title ascending, then movie_id ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC) AS rnk FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.genre1 IN ('Action','Crime','Animation')) SELECT genre1, rnk, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE rnk <= 3 ORDER BY genre1, rnk, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id
hints: RANK gives tied rows the same number, so two movies with the same avg_rating both become rank 1 and the next distinct rating jumps to rank 3. That means a genre can return more than three rows here. The window ORDER BY stays avg_rating-only on purpose; the deterministic ordering is done in the outer ORDER BY.

WITH ranked AS (SELECT ..., RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC) AS rnk FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.genre1 IN ('Action','Crime','Animation')) SELECT genre1, rnk, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE rnk <= 3 ORDER BY genre1, rnk, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id.

```sql
WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC) AS rnk FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.genre1 IN ('Action','Crime','Animation')) SELECT genre1, rnk, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE rnk <= 3 ORDER BY genre1, rnk, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id
```

exercises.prompt: Use DENSE_RANK to walk rating tiers, not row positions. Over Crime and Adventure movies with at least 50 ratings, compute DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY avg_rating DESC) — tied ratings share a tier and, unlike RANK, the next distinct rating is the very next tier (no gaps). Keep tiers 3 or lower. Return genre1, drnk, title, avg_rating, and num_ratings, ordered by genre1 ascending, then drnk ascending, then num_ratings descending, then title ascending, then movie_id ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC) AS drnk FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IN ('Crime','Adventure')) SELECT genre1, drnk, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE drnk <= 3 ORDER BY genre1, drnk, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id
hints: DENSE_RANK counts distinct rating values, so drnk <= 3 keeps every movie whose rating is one of the genre's top three distinct averages — which can be several movies per tier. Contrast with RANK, which would leave gaps after ties.

WITH ranked AS (SELECT ..., DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC) AS drnk FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IN ('Crime','Adventure')) SELECT genre1, drnk, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE drnk <= 3 ORDER BY genre1, drnk, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id.

```sql
WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC) AS drnk FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IN ('Crime','Adventure')) SELECT genre1, drnk, title, avg_rating, num_ratings FROM ranked WHERE drnk <= 3 ORDER BY genre1, drnk, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id
```

exercises.prompt: Find each genre's most-reviewed movie — popularity, not quality. Over every movie with a non-null genre1, number movies with ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY num_ratings DESC, avg_rating DESC, title, movie_id) inside a CTE and keep number 1. Return genre1, title, num_ratings, and avg_rating, ordered by genre1 ascending. (No minimum-ratings floor here — you are looking for the crowd favorite, whatever it scored.)
exercises.referenceSql: WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.num_ratings DESC, r.avg_rating DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS rn FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE m.genre1 IS NOT NULL) SELECT genre1, title, num_ratings, avg_rating FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1 ORDER BY genre1
hints: Same crown-the-winner pattern, but the window's ORDER BY leads with num_ratings DESC instead of avg_rating — the ranking key is what changes the question from 'best' to 'most-rated'.

WITH ranked AS (SELECT ..., ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.num_ratings DESC, r.avg_rating DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS rn FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE m.genre1 IS NOT NULL) SELECT genre1, title, num_ratings, avg_rating FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1 ORDER BY genre1.

```sql
WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.num_ratings DESC, r.avg_rating DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS rn FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE m.genre1 IS NOT NULL) SELECT genre1, title, num_ratings, avg_rating FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1 ORDER BY genre1
```

exercises.prompt: Rank movies two ways at once. Over movies with at least 50 ratings and a non-null genre1, compute two windows in one CTE: overall_rank = RANK() OVER (ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) across the whole set, and genre_rank = RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) within each genre. Keep the top 15 overall and return overall_rank, genre1, genre_rank, title, and avg_rating, ordered by overall_rank ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS overall_rank, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS genre_rank FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL) SELECT overall_rank, genre1, genre_rank, title, avg_rating FROM ranked WHERE overall_rank <= 15 ORDER BY overall_rank
hints: You can compute several window functions in the same SELECT — one with no PARTITION (global rank) and one partitioned by genre1 (rank within genre). Both use the same full tiebreak so each rank is a distinct integer. Filter on the overall rank in the outer query.

WITH ranked AS (SELECT ..., RANK() OVER (ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS overall_rank, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS genre_rank FROM ... WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL) SELECT overall_rank, genre1, genre_rank, title, avg_rating FROM ranked WHERE overall_rank <= 15 ORDER BY overall_rank.

```sql
WITH ranked AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.genre1, m.title, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS overall_rank, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY m.genre1 ORDER BY r.avg_rating DESC, r.num_ratings DESC, m.title, m.movie_id) AS genre_rank FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL) SELECT overall_rank, genre1, genre_rank, title, avg_rating FROM ranked WHERE overall_rank <= 15 ORDER BY overall_rank
```
